what_it_does: A static QA page that loads ../Counterpart Studio.html inside three fixed-width iframes labeled iPhone (390 px), tablet (768 px), and laptop (1024 px) so the same page can be viewed side-by-side at common breakpoints.
observations: Uses three iframe elements with explicit width/height of 390x800, 768x800, and 1024x800
Each iframe loads the same relative parent page via src='../Counterpart Studio.html'
The harness itself is a dark-themed HTML page with labeled device names above each frame
No JavaScript, no controls, and no dynamic resizing logic is present
The iframe height is fixed at 800 px for all viewports
The page includes a viewport meta tag and basic flex layout for the harness chrome
standouts: Triple fixed-width viewport preview of a single target page
Purely static HTML/CSS fixture for responsive visual QA
Relative path reference to the parent page being tested
conventions: Separates structure and presentation with inline CSS
Uses a relative file path to the target page
Labels each preview with a human-readable device name
Breaks true responsive testing by hardcoding iframe dimensions rather than using actual device emulation or dynamic resizing
concerns: The src path contains a space (Counterpart Studio.html) which may cause URL-encoding issues in some contexts
Fixed 800 px iframe height can clip or misrepresent vertically scrolling content
No error handling if the target page fails to load
Hardcoded breakpoints may not match the project's actual responsive breakpoints
No mechanism to update widths based on real device viewports
notes: A simple, zero-dependency side-by-side responsive preview harness for manually checking the parent page at three fixed widths.
questions: What content does ../Counterpart Studio.html contain and how complex is its responsive behavior?
Are 390 px, 768 px, and 1024 px the official project breakpoints?
Is this harness meant for manual visual QA, automated testing, or design handoff review?
Why was a fixed height of 800 px chosen instead of matching each device's typical viewport height?
purpose_vs_actual: The title 'Responsive harness' matches the file's purpose: it presents the same page at multiple fixed widths to preview responsive layouts, though it does not dynamically respond to viewport changes itself.
intention_signals: It appears designed to give designers or developers a quick manual way to see how the parent page renders at representative mobile, tablet, and laptop widths without relying on browser dev tools.
novelty: It is a lightweight, file-local iframe harness rather than a browser-based device emulator or third-party responsive testing service.
contribution: Contributes a self-contained QA preview pattern that records the intended breakpoint widths and a side-by-side comparison layout, which a fused system could reuse for visual regression or design handoff checks.
summary_for_embedding: A static HTML/CSS QA page that loads ../Counterpart Studio.html inside three fixed-size iframes at 390 px, 768 px, and 1024 px widths for side-by-side responsive preview.
intention_for_embedding: Intended as a manual responsive-design preview harness for checking the parent page across common device widths.
